candidates.verdict: already production; recall gain real, fp still high

candidates.config: Qwen3 flags -> Qwen3 skeptic must name a failing input or drop
candidates.finding: the skeptic upholds the flagger's own FPs — confident hallucination survives self-interrogation; every surviving FP is on a live-mined case (real loop diffs), so this is exactly the production failure mode, not a corpus artifact. Also cost one true defect (recall 1.00->0.88).

candidates.config: Qwen3 flags -> 14b (different family, fp 0.0) audits each finding
candidates.finding: The 14b's fp 0.00 comes from never flagging; as a skeptic that same conservatism drops EVERY finding, including all 8 real defects. Mirror image of self-audit (which keeps its own hallucinations). Confirms the recall/precision tradeoff is fundamental to fleet-local models.

design_conclusion: No fleet model, under any single-prompt or self-audit config tested, both catches defects and stays quiet on correct code (threshold recall>=0.75 AND fp<=0.25). The reviewer layer should therefore be ADVISORY (non-blocking) rather than gating until either (a) a cross-model skeptic is tried (different family auditing Qwen3's findings) or (b) a frontier reviewer is used for supervised runs (PXX_REVIEW_BACKEND=claude already exists). The deterministic gates + hidden eval checks remain the load-bearing independent layer regardless. CONFIRMED by the cross-model skeptic (2026-07-17): neither self-audit nor cross-audit rescues a gating reviewer — the quiet models drop truths, the sharp model hallucinates. For ADVISORY use the optimal config is the high-recall flagger SOLO (Qwen3-Coder, recall 1.0) — both skeptic layers reduce advisory value by trading away recall. Skeptic thread CLOSED; deterministic gates remain the only trustworthy enforcement.
next_experiments: advisory review mode: findings surfaced, never blocking
frontier reviewer on the same suite for an upper-bound reference
